indicator_text: Statutory/official definition of "lobbying" — narrow technical legal definition vs. broad activity-based definition
section_or_category: Terms and definitions: lobbying and lobbyist
scoring_rule: Mihut contrasts the narrow US LDA definition (covering "lobbying contacts and efforts in support of such contacts" with covered legislative/executive officials) against the broad EU ETI definition ("activities carried out with the objective of influencing the policy formulation and decision-making processes of the European institutions"). No score; descriptive contrast.
source_quote: The Lobbying Disclosure Act of 1995 (LDA) defines 'lobbying activities' as 'lobbying contacts and efforts in support of such contacts, including preparation and planning activities, research and other background work […]'; further down it defines 'lobbying contact': 'any oral or written communication (including an electronic communication) to a covered executive branch official or a covered legislative branch official […]'. Obviously, this definition is much narrower than many scholarly definitions: it does not include the influence exerted on the courts, nor the grassroots lobbying. […] When the Commission decided to approach this issue a very broad definition was chosen, as compared to the very specific, even technical American legal definition. The ETI documents defined lobbying as 'activities carried out with the objective of influencing the policy formulation and decision-making processes of the European institutions' [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:96-99, 281-283]
notes: Mihut quotes OECD 2007 stressing "the critical importance of clear definitions of those who are 'in' and, equally, those who are 'out'" but does not score the definition.

indicator_text: Statutory/official definition of "lobbyist" / "interest representative"
scoring_rule: Mihut notes the EU shift to "interest representatives" as an equivalent for "lobbyists" and lists EU-recognized organization types (public affairs consultancies, law firms, NGOs, think-tanks, corporate lobby units, trade associations).
source_quote: the documents recently adopted by the Commission as well as by the Parliament use the term 'interest representatives' as an equivalent for 'lobbyists'. […] lobbyists as 'persons carrying out such activities, working in a variety of organizations such as public affairs consultancies, law firms, NGOs, think-tanks, corporate lobby units (in-house representatives") or trade associations'" [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:55-58, 285-289]
notes: Comparative descriptive dimension; not a rubric item.

indicator_text: Branches of government covered (legislative / executive / judicial / grassroots)
scoring_rule: Mihut notes the LDA covers legislative + executive but not courts or grassroots; she does not propose a score.
source_quote: Now, the term describes the activity aimed to influence the policy-making not only in the legislature, but in the executive branch as well, and sometimes in the courts. […] this definition is much narrower than many scholarly definitions: it does not include the influence exerted on the courts, nor the grassroots lobbying. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:85-92, 96-99]

indicator_text: Regulatory approach: mandatory/compulsory vs. self-regulation vs. accreditation vs. voluntary register
section_or_category: Lobbying in the US and the EU: developments
scoring_rule: Mihut contrasts the "compulsory system typical of the American approach" against "the lower regulated system specific to the European Parliament" and "the self-regulatory approach that is still proper to the European Commission." She notes the Commission's 2007/2008 ETI as a "departure" toward a binding code of conduct + voluntary register.
source_quote: The compulsory system typical of the American approach is compared to the lower regulated system specific to the European Parliament, as well as to the self-regulatory approach that is still proper to the European Commission, even though its recent decisions indicate a departure from it. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:12-15]

indicator_text: Whether registration of lobbyists is required (and with whom)
scoring_rule: Mihut describes US LDA registration with Secretary of Senate / Clerk of House; EU Parliament accreditation via quaestor-issued passes + signed register; Commission voluntary register opened June 2008.
source_quote: lobbyists had to register with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House, and to make semiannual reports on their activities. […] the lobbyists have access to the Parliament using nominative passes issued by quaestors; in return, they are required to observe a ten-point code of conduct, and to sign a register, which is published on the Parliament website. […] the Commission […] launched the ETI in order to review the framework for 'interest representation (lobbying)'. […] a new voluntary register of interest representatives coupled with a binding code of conduct. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:353-356, 399-410, 462-470]

indicator_text: Content of disclosure reports — clients, branches/agencies lobbied, compensation, funding sources
scoring_rule: Mihut summarizes LDA disclosure (clients, house/agency lobbied, amount paid) and contrasts it with the Commission's "selected budget figures and budget sources" vs. the Parliament's call for "full financial disclosure."
source_quote: They had to report who their clients were, what house of the Congress or what agencies they lobbied, how much they were paid. […] while the Commission concluded to request registrants selected budget figures and budget sources, the Parliament called for 'full financial disclosure'. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:356-359, 452-456]

indicator_text: Reporting frequency — semiannual vs. quarterly
scoring_rule: Mihut notes the 2007 HLOGA shifted LDA reporting from semiannual to quarterly; no comparable EU frequency is set out.
source_quote: Title II, 'Full Public Disclosure of Lobbying', provides quarterly reports, rather than semiannual ones, and other detailed disclosures. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:376-378]

indicator_text: Restrictions on gifts from lobbyists to officials
scoring_rule: Mihut describes the 1995 LDA's gift limits and the 2007 HLOGA's outright prohibition on lobbyist gifts; no EU equivalent enumerated.
source_quote: the law restricted gifts to officials. […] Also, the Act prohibits giving of gifts by lobbyists and their clients to members of Congress and their staff. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:359-360, 379-381]

indicator_text: Cooling-off / revolving-door restrictions
scoring_rule: Mihut highlights HLOGA Title I "Closing the Revolving Doors" — Senate two-year cooling off (up from one); House one year. No EU equivalent identified.
source_quote: Title I of the new law is significant: 'Closing the Revolving Doors'. According to its provisions, the departing members of the Senate must wait now two years, instead of one, before lobbying former colleagues (the former members of the House of Representatives must wait one year). [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:369-375]

indicator_text: Code of conduct for lobbyists / for legislators
scoring_rule: Mihut notes the EP's ten-point code of conduct for lobbyists + Annex I code for MEPs ("Transparency and members' financial interests"); Commission's binding code of conduct (2007/2008 ETI); UK House of Commons rules "to regulate the lobbied rather than the lobbyists."
source_quote: Annex I refers to the 'Transparency and members' financial interests'; the other concerns 'Lobbying in Parliament' (Annex IX). According to this document, the lobbyists […] are required to observe a ten-point code of conduct […]. […] a new voluntary register of interest representatives coupled with a binding code of conduct. […] the House of Commons had introduced rules relating the conduct of its members, that is 'to regulate the lobbied rather than the lobbyists' [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:339-342, 397-402, 410-411]

indicator_text: Civil monetary penalties for non-compliance
scoring_rule: Mihut cites HLOGA raising civil penalties from $50,000 to $200,000.
source_quote: civil penalties are raised from $50,000 to $200,000 [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:383-385]

indicator_text: Criminal penalties for non-compliance
scoring_rule: Mihut cites HLOGA's first-time criminal penalties: imprisonment up to five years and/or specified fine.
source_quote: for the first time, criminal penalties are provided, namely imprisonment for up to five years or a specified fine, or both [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:385-388]

indicator_text: EU sanctions for non-registration
scoring_rule: Mihut notes the Commission's sanctions are "temporary suspension or exclusion from the Register" with open question whether these will deter non-compliance.
source_quote: if the automatic alert will provide a sufficient incentive for voluntary registration and if the sanctions consisting in temporary suspension or exclusion from the Register will be strong enough to prevent non-compliance. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:469-473]

indicator_text: Political Action Committees / channels for money in election campaigns
scoring_rule: Mihut flags PACs as a US-specific institution with no EU equivalent, but notes "it doesn't mean the absence of the scandals regarding the involvement of money in the electoral campaigns."
source_quote: the US developed an original organization by which corporations and various interest groups collect and spend money for the candidates' campaigns: Political Action Committees (PACs). Unlike the US, in the European countries, as well as at the European level, such a practice is neither recognized nor regulated. […] The absence of PACs in the European politics is relevant for comparison [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:165-170, 287-292]
notes: Environmental dimension, not a regulatory line-item.

indicator_text: Interest-group system: pluralism vs. corporatism vs. mixed
scoring_rule: Mihut treats the pluralism/corporatism dichotomy as a foundational comparative axis and describes the EU as "mixed" / co-existence of pluralism and neocorporatism.
source_quote: the differences between these two systems are described in terms of pluralism vs. corporatism (Lijphart 1999). […] the 'co-existence and complementarity of pluralism and neocorporatism' as a specific feature of the EU uniqueness (Michalowitz 2002: 51). [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:191-195, 240-244]
notes: Environmental dimension; structural framing of the comparison.

indicator_text: Number/character of access points (federal/multi-level governance)
scoring_rule: Mihut argues EU multi-level governance creates more access points than the US federal government, but with limited jurisdiction at the EU level.
source_quote: the European institutionalized multi-level governance created a system of multiple access points, even more than the American federal government. This is not necessarily an advantage as successful lobbying often requires having in view multiple targets because of the limited jurisdiction of the European institutions. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:259-265]

indicator_text: Professionalization of lobbying (contract lobbyists, firms, new techniques)
scoring_rule: Mihut cites Thomas 1993: US most advanced in contract-lobbyist numbers/professionalism and in new techniques (grassroots mobilization, targeted mass mailing, PR/media campaigns).
source_quote: the US is the most advanced system under both aspects: the number and the professionalism level of contract lobbyists and lobbying firms have increased substantially; also, the rise of new techniques, like mobilizing grassroots support networking, targeted mass mailing, public relations and media campaigns, etc is unparalleled in other countries (Thomas 1993: 39-40). [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:303-313]

indicator_text: Pross 2007 OECD typology cited (regulation as response to globalization vs. system-specific values)
scoring_rule: Cited; not re-derived. Pross identifies "two opposing aspects of the trend toward heightened lobby regulation."
source_quote: A.P. Pross identified two opposing aspects of the trend toward heightened lobby regulation: 'Globalization has diffused modes of lobbying across nations, creating common problems and raising similar issues in diverse societies. But, at the same time each political system values the objectives of regulation differently and varies legislative provisions accordingly' (Pross, 2007: 40). [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:319-328]
notes: Mihut invokes Pross 2007 (OECD GOV/PGC/ETH(2007)4) as background framing — not as a scoring instrument she applies.

indicator_text: Chari & Murphy 2006 IPA report typology — three-fold classification of regulatory systems (lowly / intermediately / highly regulated)
scoring_rule: Cited and quoted verbatim; not re-derived. "Lowly regulated systems […] detail who is engaged in lobbying government officials and elected representatives and getting paid for it. Intermediately regulated systems go further and report on what activity lobbying takes place in and has significant spending disclosures. Highly regulated systems go further again and state who employ lobbyists while having spending disclosure, which are open to the public" — applied: ~half of US states = highly regulated; remaining states + US federal + Canada (federal & provinces) = intermediately regulated; Germany + European Parliament = lowly regulated.
source_quote: A subsequent report […] classified the regulatory systems into thee categories: 'Lowly regulated systems in essence detail who is engaged in lobbying government officials and elected representatives and getting paid for it. Intermediately regulated systems go further and report on what activity lobbying takes place in and has significant spending disclosures. Highly regulated systems go further again and state who employ lobbyists while having spending disclosure, which are open to the public' (Chari and Murphy 2006: 89). According to the authors of this report, only about half of the American states can be classified as highly regulated; the remaining states and also the American federal level, as well as Canada, both at the federal and provinces levels come into the intermediately regulated category, while Germany and the European Parliament belong to the lowly regulated category. [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:299-320]
notes: The closest thing in this paper to a "scored" instrument — but it is *cited from* Chari & Murphy 2006, not constructed by Mihut.

indicator_text: Malone 2004 IPA report — formal regulation as "more the exception than the rule"
scoring_rule: Cited; provides the country exemplars (US, Canada as "notable exceptions"; Germany, UK as looser-regulation EU cases). No rubric structure imported.
source_quote: A report published by the Irish Institute of Public Administration revealed that formal regulation of lobbying is 'more the exception than the rule' (Malone 2004: 3). US and Canada were identified as the most notable exceptions, while within the EU looser formal regulations were found in Germany […] and in the UK [papers/text/Mihut_2008__lobbying_us_eu.txt:330-342]
